question.label: Week three check-in
question.name: What's been most on your mind at work lately, Sam?
question.description: Safe opener that lets Sam set the context before any friction is raised.

answer: I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.
note: He can execute tasks but is second-guessing whether his approach matches team expectations, which signals mild uncertainty and strain around fit.

question.label: Team expectations
question.name: When you second-guess your approach, what expectations feel hardest to read?
question.description: Drills into the specific expectation gap he just named so the manager can make it clearer.

answer: Local setup is better now, but I still lose time when the design and API details do not match.
note: He names a specific expectation mismatch between design and API details that is costing time, indicating a mild clarity gap.

question.label: Design/API mismatch
question.name: When design and API details do not match, where does it usually break first?
question.description: Drills into the specific mismatch he named so the manager can locate the unclear handoff.

answer: I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.
note: He names a specific approval-path uncertainty before pushing, which is a mild negative clarity signal; [THREAD-DEFERRED] 'who should approve what before I push' not followed because drill cap requires advancing the arc.

question.label: Done means what
question.name: What does 'done' mean to you on the tickets you've picked up so far?
question.description: Checks whether his mental model of completion matches the handoff expectations.

answer: I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first.
note: He describes delaying asks because he is trying to self-solve first, which signals an unclear threshold for when to seek help.

question.label: Thread follow
question.name: usually wait long — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
question.description: Runtime thread-follow injected because planner did not follow substantive answer.

answer: When feedback is specific and fast, I move much quicker.
note: He names a specific condition for moving faster—specific, fast feedback—showing clear clarity friction, with some engagement preserved because he responds quickly when support is timely.

question.label: Specific fast feedback
question.name: Specific and fast feedback — where is that missing most right now?
question.description: Drills into the exact point in his workflow where feedback speed or specificity is breaking down.

answer: I still feel fuzzy on where quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.
note: He names a specific clarity gap in quality expectations between quick fixes and core flows, which likely dampens confidence and momentum; [THREAD-DEFERRED] quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.

question.label: Ship-time hesitation
question.name: What tends to make shipping feel risky right before you send it?
question.description: Gets at the specific trigger for late reassurance-seeking and hesitation.

answer: Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.
note: Deficiency-as-request: he names regular pairing as currently missing support, implying both a clarity gap and insufficient growth support in how he learns to ship safely. [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent him getting stuck silently.

question.label: Tooling friction
question.name: What part of your workflow is slowing you down most right now?
question.description: Surfaces process or tooling friction that may be masquerading as confidence issues.

answer: If we define what good looks like for my next two tickets, I will feel much steadier.
note: Names a specific missing condition — a defined quality bar for the next two tickets — which is a strong clarity gap and blocked growth signal.

question.label: Next-step focus
question.name: Given what we've covered, what would you want to tighten up first?
question.description: Closes by turning the discussion into a concrete near-term focus without introducing new topics.

answer: I want to start by pairing on my next ticket so I can see the approval bar in practice.
note: He names a concrete next step—pairing on the next ticket to see the approval bar in practice—which shows usable clarity and learning intent.
